title: The Plot Against the Lord
subtitle: The priests and Pharisees gather in council to put Jesus to death

stillness: Be still before the council of the malicious.
He did not resist them by power, but chose to yield by patience.
Enter that silence, and find rest for your soul.
gospel: When the miracle of the raising of Lazarus had been made known,
the chief priests and the Pharisees gathered a council against Jesus.
They said: What do we do, for this man works many signs?
If we let him go on thus, all will believe in him;
and the Romans will come, and take away our place and our nation.
Then Caiaphas, being high priest that year, said to them:
It is expedient that one man die for the people,
and that the whole nation perish not.
This he said not of himself,
but being high priest that year he prophesied
that Jesus should die for the nation;
and not only for the nation,
but to gather into one the children of God who were dispersed.
behold: Behold: the priests, of greater authority and more learned,
and the Pharisees, who seemed more religious than the rest —
moved by anger and envy at his fame and his glory.
See them gathered, conciliated into one evil consent against Jesus.
This was the first council they held against him.
Before, they sought to kill him in secret;
now they fix the sentence, and openly treat of his death.
They saw Christ honored by the people as a king,
and they feared the Romans would count them rebels:
And the Romans will come against us, and take away our place and our nation.
They took counsel for themselves, yet would not believe.
They trembled with fear where there was no fear.
consider: These lost men plotted more how to harm Christ and destroy him
than how to take counsel for themselves, that they not perish.
Had they believed Christ, had they not killed him,
they would have lost neither their place nor their nation.
But because they did not fear to kill Christ,
they lost both their place and their nation.
They feared to lose the things of time, and did not weigh the eternal;
and so they lost them both.
The very things they did to escape, by doing them, they did not escape.
What did it profit you, O senseless Jews, to commit so great a crime?
The passion of envy and the fear of losing place and nation
overturned in them the judgment of reason;
for in no case is it lawful to kill the innocent and the just.
And consider how many there are today, even wearing the religious habit,
who, imitating the Jews, dread to lose the things of time
and do not attend to the eternal —
and so, not without cause, they lose them both.
Because now, with the Jews, they love the inner darkness, the darkness of guilt,
they shall then be cast with them into the outer darkness, the darkness of hell.
The covetous and the vain religious is a thing to be lamented,
more wretched in a way than all creatures —
for he is left without God and without the world.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, Angel of great counsel,
who permitted the council of the malicious to be formed against you,
and willed not to resist them by power, but rather to yield by patience:
do not abandon me in erring judgment,
nor in the harmful counsel of my own will or of men,
nor in the power or temptation of demons;
but you yourself, gently, within and without, order and rule me,
and direct me into the way of eternal salvation.
Grant me, Lord, that whatever adversities of devil or of men contrive against me
so that, unharmed and snatched from every peril,
I may joyfully render to you my thanksgiving. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Temporalia perdere timuerunt, et æterna non cogitaverunt, et sic utraque amiserunt.
latinJewel.english: They feared to lose the things of time, and did not weigh the eternal; and so they lost them both.
